Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies deployment-efficient reward-free exploration problems in the linear MDP setting. The authors propose an algorithm that can achieve optimal deployment efficiency.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:

The theoretical results seem to be sound although I did not check all the details. The writing is clear and the discussion about prior work is sufficient.

Weakness:

I think this paper is a combination of several existing RL theory works. Reward-free exploration problems with linear function approximation have been studied in the literature. Deployment-efficient (similar to low-switching cost RL) problems have also been studied. This paper combines all the ingredients and resolves some additional technical difficulties to obtain the new results. This is good but I feel the novelty is not significant.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
I think the paper is overall well-written. The results appear to be new in the literature, although not novel enough.

Summary Of The Review:
I think this paper is good overall. I did not check the details of the proof so I don't have high confidence in my assessment.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the problem of deployment efficient reinforcement learning with linear function approximation under the reward-free exploration setting. The authors propose a new algorithm that achieves optimal deployment complexity and near-optimal sample complexity at the same time. Their techniques include an exploration-preserving policy discretization and a generalized G-optimal experiment design, which makes incremental contributions to the RL theory community.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength
S1: The proposed algorithm achieves SOTA sample complexity and preserves optimal deployment complexity simultaneously.
S2: The proposed exploration-preserving policy discretization looks novel.

Weakness
S1: The proposed algorithm appears to be only making limited improvement compared to existing works in (Huang et al. 2022). Specifically, the paper considered a well-formulated dedicated problem, yet only makes a factor of d improvement on sample complexity, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022). Besides, the proposed algorithm is computationally inefficient.
S2: The technical contribution of this paper is limited and below the threshold of ICLR. The basic algorithm framework looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022), except upgrading the policy set and estimating the value functions instead of optimism, as pointed out in Appendix G. Besides, the idea of estimating the value functions instead of optimism has already been proposed in (Chen et al. 2021) for reward-free exploration in linear mixture MDPs.
S3: The overall writing is not very clear such that readers are hard to follow the technical points, while this paper focuses on algorithm techniques. It is strongly recommended that authors could provide more details about G-optimal design in the main body of the paper.

(Chen et al. 2021) Chen, Xiaoyu, Jiachen Hu, Lin F. Yang, and Liwei Wang. "Near-optimal reward-free exploration for linear mixture mdps with plug-in solver." arXiv preprint arXiv:2110.03244 (2021).

Comments and Questions
C1: The writing of the introduction looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022) and does not provide much further information.
C2: The linear MDP definition in (Jin et al., 2020b)’ paper is different from this paper. There $\mu_h$ is a matrix, not a vector.
C3: Assumption 2.1 is very strong and limits the generality of the algorithm, as those in (Zanette et al., 2020b; Wagenmaker & Jamieson, 2022). Since under linear MDP, if some direction is hard to encounter, we do not necessarily need to gather much information on this direction. Besides, I do not find analogous assumptions in (Huang et al., 2022).
C4: It is more common to say different “stages” of $h$, instead of “layer”
C5: The full-text layout is poor since the authors manually decrease the margin between paragraphs and sections. For example, there is an overlapping between the text and the footnote line.
C6: In the paragraph below Theorem 7.1, what is the “mild assumption” to recover the best-known result from (Qiao et al. 2022).
C7: As stated in Section 7.2, why the computational issue of the algorithm is usually not considered as a fundamental barrier? A computationally-inefficient algorithm largely hurts the significance of the proposed algorithm.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The writing of the paper can be improved. The authors should also further highlight the novelty of their results, in particular, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022).

Although some parts of the algorithm and proof are novel, the proposed algorithm is computationally-inefficient, and the proposed techniques are dedicated to the DE linear MDP. Thus, the overall contributions are marginally below the ICLR threshold.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the problem of deployment efficient reinforcement learning with linear function approximation under the reward-free exploration setting. The authors propose a new algorithm that achieves optimal deployment complexity and near-optimal sample complexity at the same time. Their techniques include an exploration-preserving policy discretization and a generalized G-optimal experiment design, which makes incremental contributions to the RL theory community.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength
S1: The proposed algorithm achieves SOTA sample complexity and preserves optimal deployment complexity simultaneously.
S2: The proposed exploration-preserving policy discretization looks novel.

Weakness
S1: The proposed algorithm appears to be only making limited improvement compared to existing works in (Huang et al. 2022). Specifically, the paper considered a well-formulated dedicated problem, yet only makes a factor of d improvement on sample complexity, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022). Besides, the proposed algorithm is computationally inefficient.
S2: The technical contribution of this paper is limited and below the threshold of ICLR. The basic algorithm framework looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022), except upgrading the policy set and estimating the value functions instead of optimism, as pointed out in Appendix G. Besides, the idea of estimating the value functions instead of optimism has already been proposed in (Chen et al. 2021) for reward-free exploration in linear mixture MDPs.
S3: The overall writing is not very clear such that readers are hard to follow the technical points, while this paper focuses on algorithm techniques. It is strongly recommended that authors could provide more details about G-optimal design in the main body of the paper.

(Chen et al. 2021) Chen, Xiaoyu, Jiachen Hu, Lin F. Yang, and Liwei Wang. "Near-optimal reward-free exploration for linear mixture mdps with plug-in solver." arXiv preprint arXiv:2110.03244 (2021).

Comments and Questions
C1: The writing of the introduction looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022) and does not provide much further information.
C2: The linear MDP definition in (Jin et al., 2020b)’ paper is different from this paper. There $\mu_h$ is a matrix, not a vector.
C3: Assumption 2.1 is very strong and limits the generality of the algorithm, as those in (Zanette et al., 2020b; Wagenmaker & Jamieson, 2022). Since under linear MDP, if some direction is hard to encounter, we do not necessarily need to gather much information on this direction. Besides, I do not find analogous assumptions in (Huang et al., 2022).
C4: It is more common to say different “stages” of $h$, instead of “layer”
C5: The full-text layout is poor since the authors manually decrease the margin between paragraphs and sections. For example, there is an overlapping between the text and the footnote line.
C6: In the paragraph below Theorem 7.1, what is the “mild assumption” to recover the best-known result from (Qiao et al. 2022).
C7: As stated in Section 7.2, why the computational issue of the algorithm is usually not considered as a fundamental barrier? A computationally-inefficient algorithm largely hurts the significance of the proposed algorithm.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The writing of the paper can be improved. The authors should also further highlight the novelty of their results, in particular, compared to that in (Huang et al. 2022).

Summary Of The Review:
The paper considers a well-formulated theoretical problem and makes incremental improvements to existing algorithms. Although some parts of the algorithm and proof are novel, the proposed algorithm is computationally-inefficient, and the proposed techniques are dedicated to the DE linear MDP. Thus, the overall contributions are marginally below the ICLR threshold.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Summary Of The Paper:**
This paper investigates the challenging problem of deployment-efficient reward-free reinforcement learning (RL) with linear function approximation. The authors propose a novel algorithm that aims to achieve optimal deployment complexity and near-optimal sample complexity simultaneously, addressing a significant theoretical objective in the field. The core technical innovations include an exploration-preserving policy discretization and a generalized G-optimal experiment design. The work also provides information-theoretic lower bounds for switching cost and batch complexity in related problems, and demonstrates its applicability to tabular MDPs.

**Strength And Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**
1.  **Significant Theoretical Objective and Novel Techniques:** The paper addresses a highly practical and well-motivated problem by aiming for simultaneous near-optimal sample complexity of \(\tilde{O}(d^2 H^5 / \epsilon^2)\) and optimal deployment complexity of H. This objective represents a notable theoretical stride, as achieving both concurrently has been a long-standing challenge. The introduction of generalized G-optimal experiment design for policy selection and exploration-preserving policy discretization are genuinely novel technical contributions. These techniques are crucial for achieving the stated bounds and represent a step forward in managing adaptivity constraints in RL. The algorithm also shows improved asymptotic independence from the explorability parameter \(\lambda^*\) in the small-\(\epsilon\) regime, which is a desirable theoretical property compared to prior work (e.g., Huang et al., 2022). The paper further provides valuable lower bounds for regret minimization under adaptivity constraints, guiding future research directions.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Limited Improvement and Computational Inefficiency:** While the paper targets an important problem and achieves strong theoretical guarantees, the proposed algorithm offers only limited improvement compared to existing works like (Huang et al. 2022), specifically a factor of \(d\) improvement on sample complexity for a dedicated problem. More critically, the proposed algorithm is computationally inefficient. The authors acknowledge that solving the core optimization problem (1) for selecting the exploration policy is generally computationally intractable. Dismissing this as "usually not considered as a fundamental barrier" for information-theoretic results is weak, especially given the paper's emphasis on practical motivation. The proposed "softmax or gradient-based optimization" is a vague suggestion lacking any guarantee of tractability or performance, which significantly limits the immediate practical deployability and relevance of the algorithm without further, non-trivial algorithmic advancements for approximation.
2.  **Marginal Technical Contribution and Critical Algorithmic Inconsistencies:** The overall technical contribution of this paper, despite some novel components, appears to be marginally below the threshold for a top-tier conference. The basic algorithm framework looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022), except for upgrading the policy set and estimating value functions instead of optimism, an idea that has already been explored in (Chen et al. 2021) for reward-free exploration in linear mixture MDPs. Furthermore, the manuscript suffers from several critical methodological inconsistencies and ambiguities that cast doubt on the precise implementation and theoretical guarantees. For instance, the parameter `A` for `EstimateER` in Algorithm 1, line 6, is stated as `A = C2d3Hι2`, which directly contradicts the more accurate and derived value `A = C2d7/2H 3ι3 / (C1ϵ)` presented in Appendix F.1. Such a discrepancy in a key algorithmic parameter is unacceptable and severely impacts the credibility of the work. Additionally, Algorithm 1, line 2, and Algorithm 2, line 2, refer to "Construct Πexp ϵ/3 as in Section 3.2" and "Construct Πeval ϵ/3 as in Section 3.2," respectively, but Section 3.2 is merely an overview, with detailed constructions relegated to Appendix C without precise cross-referencing for critical algorithmic components.
3.  **Clarity and Rigor Issues:** The overall writing is not very clear, making it difficult for readers to follow the technical points, despite the paper's focus on algorithmic techniques. There is a strong recommendation that authors provide more details about the generalized G-optimal design in the main body. Beyond general clarity, specific instances of imprecision detract from the paper's rigor. The claim in Section 5 that "a small modification to an intermediate argument gives the same \(\tilde{O}(S^2AH^5 / \epsilon^2)\)" for tabular MDPs (Theorem 7.1) is problematic; a "small modification" yielding a factor of `A` improvement in sample complexity is far from trivial and warrants detailed explanation or an explicit proof. The current treatment in Appendix H.1, stating "with minor revision... we sketch the proof... while ignoring details," is insufficient for such a pivotal claim. Moreover, the use of `O` versus `\(\tilde{O}\)` is not perfectly consistent throughout the main text, despite `\(\tilde{O}\)` being defined. Footnote 7 lists C1, C2, C3 as universal constants in Algorithm 1, but C4, also a universal constant, is introduced in Theorem 5.1 and used in the proof without being explicitly listed alongside the others. These minor but numerous inconsistencies detract from the meticulousness expected in a high-quality theoretical paper.
4.  **Strong Assumptions and Formatting:** Assumption 2.1, requiring explorability of all directions, is a very strong requirement that limits the generality of the algorithm, similar to limitations noted in (Zanette et al., 2020b; Wagenmaker & Jamieson, 2022). This assumption is not explicitly present in (Huang et al., 2022). Furthermore, the writing of the introduction looks similar to that in (Huang et al. 2022) and does not provide much further information. The linear MDP definition in (Jin et al., 2020b)’ paper is different from this paper, where \(\mu_h\) is a matrix, not a vector. Finally, the full-text layout is poor, with authors manually decreasing the margin between paragraphs and sections, leading to issues like overlapping text and footnote lines. It is also more common to say different “stages” of \(h\), instead of “layer”.

**Comments and Questions:**
*   In the paragraph below Theorem 7.1, what is the “mild assumption” to recover the best-known result from (Qiao et al. 2022)? This needs to be clarified.

**Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:**
**Clarity:** The paper's overall structure is comprehensible, and the introduction clearly sets the stage. However, the clarity is significantly marred by the aforementioned methodological inconsistencies, imprecise cross-referencing for critical algorithmic components, vague explanations for key modifications, and general density of the theoretical arguments, especially in the appendices. More intuitive explanations or intermediate steps in the main body would improve readability for a broader audience.
**Quality:** The theoretical quality of the core contributions is high, with strong guarantees for the proposed algorithm. The underlying mathematical derivations, as far as can be ascertained from the provided text and proof sketches, appear sound. However, the lack of meticulousness in algorithmic parameterization, the insufficient rigor in explaining critical modifications, and the numerous inconsistencies detract from the overall quality expected of a top-tier submission.
**Novelty:** The paper introduces genuinely novel techniques, such as the generalized G-optimal design and exploration-preserving policy discretization, to achieve simultaneous near-optimal complexities, which is a significant advancement. However, the overall algorithmic framework and the incremental nature of the improvements compared to very recent prior work (e.g., Huang et al. 2022, Chen et al. 2021) suggest that the comprehensive novelty is not as substantial as initially perceived. The authors should further highlight the novelty of their results, especially compared to (Huang et al. 2022).
**Reproducibility:** For a theoretical paper, the reproducibility of the theoretical results hinges on the correctness and verifiability of the proofs, which are detailed in the appendices. However, the direct reproducibility of the algorithm in a practical setting is questionable due to the unaddressed computational inefficiency of solving the core optimization problem (1) and the lack of explicit universal constants. The vaguely described "small modification" for tabular MDPs also impedes confidence in reproducing those specific results.

**Summary Of The Review:**
This paper considers a well-formulated theoretical problem, making some notable theoretical advancements in achieving simultaneous optimal deployment and near-optimal sample complexities in reward-free linear MDPs. The proposed techniques, including generalized G-optimal experiment design and exploration-preserving policy discretization, offer genuinely novel ideas. However, the manuscript is plagued by several critical methodological inconsistencies in algorithmic parameterization, insufficient rigor in explaining key modifications, and a general lack of clarity in technical details. Combined with the acknowledged computational inefficiency and the incremental nature of improvements over existing work, the overall contributions are marginally below the ICLR threshold. Substantial revisions addressing the precision, clarity, and rigor issues would be necessary to elevate the paper's standing.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Disentangling Learning Representations with Density Estimation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper considers the problem of disentangling representations, where we aim to learn representations that have independent features. Authors start with the common learning objective that encourages disentanglement, the Total Correlation (TC), and hypothesize that an important disadvantage of this objective is that it requires an estimate of the latent density. This density is difficult to compute for high-dimensional representations due to the curse of dimensionality. Authors propose an alternative objective, Excess Entropy Power (EEP), that combines TC with Dual Total Correlation (DTC), and can be computed using only the estimates of _conditional_ densities in the latent space. Such conditional densities are often easier to estimate, as also shown by authors in a toy experiment. Authors run experiments on two datasets with known latent factors, observing that EEP improves upon strong VAE-based baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The observation that TC depends on the quality of the full density estimate is a keen one, and the solution based on combining TC with DTC is non-trivial and novel.
- The proposed method is introduced in great detail. The mathematical notation is clear and consistent.
- Experiments use strong baselines: $\beta$-VAE and its variants. The proposed method consistently outperforms these baselines.
- Empirical studies of the role of hyperparameters $\sigma$ and $\lambda$.
- A good critical discussion of the results, as well as the limitations of the method.

Weaknesses:
- Related work limited to a single paragraph in the introduction. I would've liked to see a broader discussion of disentanglement methods, representation learning methods, and density estimation methods. For density estimation, especially methods that model the density as a sequence of conditional distributions (autoregressive density estimators).
- The KL divergence values are sometimes _negative_ in Figure 1(b): how is this possible?
- Several choices in the paper are not clearly motivated, including the density ratio estimation as the density estimation method, the loss weighting scheme in equation 3, and the autoencoder with Gaussian noise as the representation learning method.
- Density ratio estimation is characterized several times as "non-parametric", even though we're parametrizing the density (albeit implicitly) via the discriminator neural net.
- I am not convinced we're comparing apples to apples in Figure 1: in (a) we're estimating an $m$-dimensional density, while in (b) we're estimating a 1-dimensional density. Estimating the $m$-dimensional density as a series of conditional densities in (b) would be more convincing.
- Authors do discuss that the method is computationally expensive (need to train $m$ discriminators), but there are no runtime metrics reported in the experiments section.

Questions:
- Have you tried using GCAE as a generative model (as you suggest in the discussion)?
- Do you have intuition for why in Figure 4, (b) part especially, the MIG is going down for $\alpha > 0.2$? Is it because we're "over-disentangling", and the model struggles to capture any info about the true factors?

Minor:
- Citation style: consider making use of `\citep` and `\citet`.
- Define $m$ in the caption of Figure 1.
- A figure on page 6 has no caption. It is also not clear what the different dots that have the same color represent.
- Define $h$ in the first equation of section 3.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The clarity and quality of the manuscript could be improved (see weaknesses above). To the best of my knowledge, the method is novel, albeit it could be related better to prior work. Reproducibility is good: authors provide the details of their training procedure.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The proposed method solves an important problem, is novel, and is evaluated well. However, several choices in the paper are not very well motivated, and the clarity and quality of the manuscript could be improved. As it stands, I consider the paper to be below the bar for acceptance, but would be willing to reconsider if the authors addressed the points raised above.

---

Authors have responded to some of my points, especially in regards to motivating the choices in the paper, and I have increased my score as a result. I believe the paper can be made stronger still by better connecting to methods in density estimation and studying the pros and cons of autoregressive density estimation in this context, but I'd now support it getting accepted.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper considers the problem of disentangling representations, where we aim to learn representations that have independent features. Authors start with the common learning objective that encourages disentanglement, the Total Correlation (TC), and hypothesize that an important disadvantage of this objective is that it requires an estimate of the latent density. This density is difficult to compute for high-dimensional representations due to the curse of dimensionality. Authors propose an alternative objective, Excess Entropy Power (EEP), that combines TC with Dual Total Correlation (DTC), and can be computed using only the estimates of _conditional_ densities in the latent space. Such conditional densities are often easier to estimate, as also shown by authors in a toy experiment. Authors run experiments on two datasets with known latent factors, observing that EEP improves upon strong VAE-based baselines.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The observation that TC depends on the quality of the full density estimate is a keen one, and the solution based on combining TC with DTC is non-trivial and novel.
- The proposed method is introduced in great detail. The mathematical notation is clear and consistent.
- Experiments use strong baselines: $\beta$-VAE and its variants. The proposed method consistently outperforms these baselines.
- Empirical studies of the role of hyperparameters $\sigma$ and $\lambda$.
- A good critical discussion of the results, as well as the limitations of the method.

Weaknesses:
- Related work limited to a single paragraph in the introduction. I would've liked to see a broader discussion of disentanglement methods, representation learning methods, and density estimation methods. For density estimation, especially methods that model the density as a sequence of conditional distributions (autoregressive density estimators).
- The KL divergence values are sometimes _negative_ in Figure 1(b): how is this possible?
- Several choices in the paper are not clearly motivated, including the density ratio estimation as the density estimation method, the loss weighting scheme in equation 3, and the autoencoder with Gaussian noise as the representation learning method.
- Density ratio estimation is characterized several times as "non-parametric", even though we're parametrizing the density (albeit implicitly) via the discriminator neural net.
- I am not convinced we're comparing apples to apples in Figure 1: in (a) we're estimating an $m$-dimensional density, while in (b) we're estimating a 1-dimensional density. Estimating the $m$-dimensional density as a series of conditional densities in (b) would be more convincing.
- Authors do discuss that the method is computationally expensive (need to train $m$ discriminators), but there are no runtime metrics reported in the experiments section.

Questions:
- Have you tried using GCAE as a generative model (as you suggest in the discussion)?
- Do you have intuition for why in Figure 4, (b) part especially, the MIG is going down for $\alpha > 0.2$? Is it because we're "over-disentangling", and the model struggles to capture any info about the true factors?

Minor:
- Citation style: consider making use of `\citep` and `\citet`.
- Define $m$ in the caption of Figure 1.
- A figure on page 6 has no caption. It is also not clear what the different dots that have the same color represent.
- Define $h$ in the first equation of section 3.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The clarity and quality of the manuscript could be improved (see weaknesses above). To the best of my knowledge, the method is novel, albeit it could be related better to prior work. Reproducibility is good: authors provide the details of their training procedure.

Summary Of The Review:
The proposed method solves an important problem, is novel, and is evaluated well. However, several choices in the paper are not very well motivated, and the clarity and quality of the manuscript could be improved. As it stands, I consider the paper to be below the bar for acceptance, but would be willing to reconsider if the authors addressed the points raised above.

---

Authors have responded to some of my points, especially in regards to motivating the choices in the paper, and I have increased my score as a result. I believe the paper can be made stronger still by better connecting to methods in density estimation and studying the pros and cons of autoregressive density estimation in this context, but I'd now support it getting accepted.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces the Gaussian Channel Autoencoder (GCAE), a novel method for learning disentangled representations. It addresses the challenge of reliably disentangling features by tackling the curse of dimensionality inherent in density estimation for high-dimensional latent spaces, a common issue in existing VAE-based approaches. Authors hypothesize that a key disadvantage of the commonly used Total Correlation (TC) objective is its reliance on an estimate of the full latent density, which is difficult to compute.

GCAE proposes an alternative objective, Excess Entropy Power (EEP), which combines TC with Dual Total Correlation (DTC). A core strength of this approach is its ability to compute the objective using only estimates of *conditional* densities in the latent space, which are often easier to estimate. This is achieved via a multi-discriminator architecture, representing the latent joint distribution as a collection of low-dimensional conditional distributions. The method also incorporates Gaussian noise in the latent space for smoothing and introduces the EEP loss for feature stabilization. Authors demonstrate in a toy experiment that conditional density estimation is more tractable.

Experiments on two datasets with known latent factors (Beamsynthesis and dSprites) show that GCAE consistently improves upon strong VAE-based baselines such as $\beta$-VAE and its variants. The method demonstrates competitive and reliable disentanglement scores across multiple metrics (MIG, Factor Score, DCI, SAP), often exhibiting lower variance than baselines, and shows notable robustness to increasing latent space dimensionality. The authors also propose LΣI as a promising unsupervised indicator for disentanglement success, noting its good correlation with MIG.

**Strengths:**

1.  **Novelty and Theoretical Motivation:** The core idea of leveraging Dual Total Correlation (DTC) and a collection of conditional distributions to overcome the curse of dimensionality in disentanglement is highly original and constitutes a significant conceptual and methodological advance. The observation that TC depends on the quality of the full density estimate is keen, and the solution based on combining TC with DTC is non-trivial and novel, offering a fresh perspective on reliable disentanglement. The theoretical argument for DTC over TC, grounded in the curse of dimensionality, is compelling and well-articulated.
2.  **Technical Soundness and Detailed Presentation:** The proposed method is introduced in great detail, with clear justifications for the DTC approach and the density-ratio trick. The mathematical notation is generally clear and consistent, guiding the reader through the problem, proposed solution, and empirical validation effectively. The integration of Gaussian noise to smooth the latent space for density estimation is a sound design choice.
3.  **Strong Empirical Performance and Robustness:** Experiments use strong baselines and demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms them, achieving superior and more robust performance. The evaluation is extensive and rigorous, comparing GCAE against four prominent baselines on two diverse datasets using four widely accepted disentanglement metrics. The results, presented with both mean scores and standard deviations, strongly support the claims of competitive performance and improved reliability, addressing a critical concern in the field. The method's robustness to latent dimension is a notable improvement over TC-based methods like FactorVAE.
4.  **Comprehensive Evaluation and Insights:** The paper includes empirical studies of the role of hyperparameters $\sigma$ and $\lambda$, and an ablation study for LEEP helps justify its contribution to stable disentanglement. Authors provide a good critical discussion of the results, as well as the limitations of the method, demonstrating transparency. The finding that LΣI correlates well with MIG offers a valuable step towards unsupervised assessment of disentanglement.
5.  **Reproducibility:** Authors provide the details of their training procedure, detailed architectural specifications, hyperparameter settings, and training procedures in the appendices, along with a statement of code availability. This commitment to detail significantly enhances the reproducibility of the reported results.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:**

While the core idea is strong and the empirical results are promising, the manuscript's presentation, particularly its technical precision, could be further refined.

1.  **Mathematical Imprecision and Ambiguity:** Several mathematical expressions and descriptions could benefit from greater precision. For instance, the density-ratio trick equation `p_fake(x) = D*(x) p_real(x) / 1-D*(x)` in Section 2 lacks necessary parentheses for clarity. The definition of the `zm` distribution in Figure 1b's description `zm ~ N(µ = (m − 1)− 1 m )` appears mathematically flawed or misstated. The claim in Section 3 that `h(Zi)` is estimated with `Ez\i pZi(zi|z\i)` is misleading, as `h(Zi)` is marginal entropy. The `1/(2πe)` factor in the LEEP loss (Eq. 3) is placed outside the summation, which is technically inconsistent with how entropy power is typically defined per component. The `1/n` scaling factor in `LGCAE` (Eq. 4) needs clarification regarding `n`'s specific role (input or output dimension).
2.  **Inconsistent Notation and Terminology:** The paper exhibits inconsistent citation styles (e.g., "Bengio et al. (2013) - it is meant to be" versus standard parenthetical citations) and inconsistent use of "et al." vs. "&" in author lists. The notation `νσ` for Gaussian noise is unconventional and confusing. The term "dual latent parameters" for VAEs in the experiment details is vague.
3.  **Inconsistencies in Methodological Details:** A critical inconsistency exists in Appendix A.1 regarding the information functional estimation, where it states `z_phi` (noiseless) is used for `z\i` in the expectation `Ez\i~phi_sigma(X)`, which contradicts the `phi_sigma(X)` notation that implies noisy `z`. This significantly impacts reproducibility. The description of "2 × Linear(512, m)" for the GCAE encoder output in Table 1 is ambiguous, making architecture understanding difficult.
4.  **Related Work Discussion:** The related work section is limited to a single paragraph in the introduction. A broader discussion of disentanglement methods, representation learning methods, and density estimation methods—especially those modeling density as a sequence of conditional distributions (autoregressive density estimators)—would strengthen the paper.
5.  **Unclear Motivations:** While some choices were clarified following author response, several choices in the paper could still benefit from clearer motivation, including the density ratio estimation as the sole density estimation method and the autoencoder with Gaussian noise as the representation learning method. The loss weighting scheme in Equation 3 could also be better motivated.
6.  **Mischaracterization of Density Estimation:** Density ratio estimation is characterized several times as "non-parametric," even though the density is parametrized (albeit implicitly) via the discriminator neural net.
7.  **Experimental Comparisons:** The comparison in Figure 1 is not entirely "apples to apples": in (a) an $m$-dimensional density is estimated, while in (b) a 1-dimensional density is estimated. Estimating the $m$-dimensional density as a series of conditional densities in (b) would be more convincing. Also, the KL divergence values are sometimes *negative* in Figure 1(b), which requires explanation.
8.  **Computational Cost and Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** The method is acknowledged to be computationally expensive (training $m$ discriminators), which could be a practical limitation for very high-dimensional latent spaces. While the method avoids the curse of dimensionality in estimation, it introduces a computational burden. Although the authors claim hyperparameter transferability, they also note that the optimization of 'm' discriminators is sensitive to learning rate and optimizer choices, suggesting potential challenges in practical application and tuning. While runtime metrics are discussed, explicit reporting in the experiments section would be beneficial.
9.  **LEEP Explanation:** While the ablation study validates LEEP's utility, a deeper theoretical or intuitive explanation of *why* weighting by entropy power leads to feature stabilization, beyond empirical observation, would enhance understanding.
10. **Formatting Issues:** Persistent hyphenation artifacts (e.g., "Gaus- sian", "la- tent", "lan- guage") and minor formatting errors (e.g., footnote marker `1` immediately after "Experiments" without a space) detract from the professional presentation.

**Questions:**

*   Have you tried using GCAE as a generative model (as you suggest in the discussion)?
*   Do you have intuition for why in Figure 4, (b) part especially, the MIG is going down for $\alpha > 0.2$? Is it because we're "over-disentangling," and the model struggles to capture any info about the true factors?

**Minor:**

*   Citation style: consider making use of `\citep` and `\citet`.
*   Define $m$ in the caption of Figure 1.
*   A figure on page 6 has no caption. It is also not clear what the different dots that have the same color represent.
*   Define $h$ in the first equation of section 3.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**

The paper's high-level narrative and overall structure are clear. However, its *technical* clarity is significantly compromised by the pervasive mathematical inaccuracies, ambiguous formulations, and inconsistent terminology, which demand significant effort from a meticulous reader to interpret. The underlying scientific contribution—the novel application of DTC and conditional density estimation—is of high quality. The experimental validation is thorough and supports the claims of competitive performance and robustness. The method is novel, offering a significant conceptual and methodological advance. Reproducibility is good, with authors providing detailed information and code availability, though the aforementioned ambiguities in the text could still make independent reproduction challenging without careful reverse-engineering of the provided code.

**Summary Of The Review:**

The proposed method, GCAE, solves an important problem in disentangled representation learning, is novel, and is evaluated well with strong empirical results demonstrating reliability and robustness. While the core idea is innovative and technically sound, the manuscript is plagued by a substantial number of minor yet impactful flaws related to mathematical precision, consistent notation, and clear methodological details. These issues, though not undermining the core contribution, significantly hinder the paper's overall clarity and rigorous presentation. Addressing these points meticulously would elevate the paper's quality further. As it stands, the paper represents a valuable contribution to the field and is marginally above the acceptance threshold, but would benefit from rigorous refinement of its presentation.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper considers the problem of disentangling representations, where we aim to learn representations that have independent features. Authors start with the common learning objective that encourages disentanglement, the Total Correlation (TC), and hypothesize that an important disadvantage of this objective is that it requires an estimate of the latent density. This density is difficult to compute for high-dimensional representations due to the curse of dimensionality. Authors propose an alternative objective, Excess Entropy Power (EEP), that combines TC with Dual Total Correlation (DTC), and can be computed using only the estimates of _conditional_ densities in the latent space. Such conditional densities are often easier to estimate, as also shown by authors in a toy experiment. Authors run experiments on two datasets with known latent factors, observing that EEP improves upon strong VAE-based baselines.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The observation that TC depends on the quality of the full density estimate is a keen one, and the solution based on combining TC with DTC is non-trivial and novel.
- The proposed method is introduced in great detail. The mathematical notation is clear and consistent.
- Experiments use strong baselines: $\beta$-VAE and its variants. The proposed method consistently outperforms these baselines.
- Empirical studies of the role of hyperparameters $\sigma$ and $\lambda$.
- A good critical discussion of the results, as well as the limitations of the method.

Weaknesses:
- Related work limited to a single paragraph in the introduction. I would've liked to see a broader discussion of disentanglement methods, representation learning methods, and density estimation methods. For density estimation, especially methods that model the density as a sequence of conditional distributions (autoregressive density estimators).
- The KL divergence values are sometimes _negative_ in Figure 1(b): how is this possible?
- Several choices in the paper are not clearly motivated, including the density ratio estimation as the density estimation method, the loss weighting scheme in equation 3, and the autoencoder with Gaussian noise as the representation learning method.
- Density ratio estimation is characterized several times as "non-parametric", even though we're parametrizing the density (albeit implicitly) via the discriminator neural net.
- I am not convinced we're comparing apples to apples in Figure 1: in (a) we're estimating an $m$-dimensional density, while in (b) we're estimating a 1-dimensional density. Estimating the $m$-dimensional density as a series of conditional densities in (b) would be more convincing.
- Authors do discuss that the method is computationally expensive (need to train $m$ discriminators), but there are no runtime metrics reported in the experiments section.

Questions:
- Have you tried using GCAE as a generative model (as you suggest in the discussion)?
- Do you have intuition for why in Figure 4, (b) part especially, the MIG is going down for $\alpha > 0.2$? Is it because we're "over-disentangling", and the model struggles to capture any info about the true factors?

Minor:
- Citation style: consider making use of `\citep` and `\citet`.
- Define $m$ in the caption of Figure 1.
- A figure on page 6 has no caption. It is also not clear what the different dots that have the same color represent.
- Define $h$ in the first equation of section 3.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The clarity and quality of the manuscript could be improved (see weaknesses above). To the best of my knowledge, the method is novel, albeit it could be related better to prior work. Reproducibility is good: authors provide the details of their training procedure.

Summary Of The Review:
The proposed method solves an important problem, is novel, and is evaluated well. However, several choices in the paper are not very well motivated, and the clarity and quality of the manuscript could be improved. As it stands, I consider the paper to be below the bar for acceptance, but would be willing to reconsider if the authors addressed the points raised above.

---

Authors have responded to some of my points, especially in regards to motivating the choices in the paper, and I have increased my score as a result. I believe the paper can be made stronger still by better connecting to methods in density estimation and studying the pros and cons of autoregressive density estimation in this context, but I'd now support it getting accepted.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper is about learning disentanglement using scalable non-parametric density estimation. The author proposes a dual total correlation (DTC) metric for disentangling that divides the joint distribution into many low-dimensional conditional distributions. The authors conduct extensive experiments to show the superiority of their approach against state-of-the-art baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength

1) The analysis is shown in section 3, which derives the DTC and its comparison against total correlation. DTC in eqn 1. removes the requirement to compute p(z). DTC requires computing the conditional entropies for disentanglement.
2) Propose a new "Gaussian Channel Autoencoder" (GCAE). As the name suggests, gaussian noise with variance sigma^2 is added to smooth the representations. And in Sec 2, the authors have detailed how they use the discriminator-based method, which applies the density-ratio trick and the Radon-Nikodym theorem to estimate the density of samples from an unknown distribution.
3) The authors conduct experiments on Beamsynthesis (time series dataset) and dsprites (synthetic images) to show GCAE is better than previous baselines. Fig 3a and 3b show the scatter plot of GCAE for summed information loss against mean squared error (MSE) and mutual information gap (MIG). GCAE performs better than baseline models for Beamsynthesis, while in the case of dsprites it does better only for a small range of lambda (hyperparameter of summed information loss).



Weakness

1) The novelty of the paper is limited. The authors claim that using DTC  removes the constraint of estimating p(z), which is difficult compared to the conditional distribution. However, computing "m" conditional distributions needs "m" discriminators, which are sensitive to hyperparameters. It also requires a lot of data and time.
2) The authors use only the MIG metric to show disentanglement. The authors should have compared against other metrics such as DCI, SAP, etc. Please refer to "Measuring Disentanglement: A Review of Metrics" to understand why there is not one disentangling metric and each metric explores different aspects.
3) The datasets used for the experiments are limited. The results work well for Beamsynthesis, not for dsprites. It can be seen in figure 4a and 4b. The scatter plot in figure 3b for beam synthesis shows a negative correlation coefficient of -0.823. There is no mention of dsprites.
4) No qualitative results were shown for dsprites and no results on the latent traversal on these z axes. Since the authors chose m=10 as the latent dimension, and dsprites has five factors of variation, the qualitative result should have helped understand how the disentanglement happened.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The work needs more finetuning and structuring. 
Example:
1) Page 6: "The tightly grouped samples in the lower right of the plot correspond with lambda = 0, and incorporating any lambda > 0 leads to a decrease in L_sumI and increase in MSE. As lambda is increased further the MSE increases only slightly as the average L_sumI decreases significantly." This line is unclear in context with lambda and figure 3a.
2) Page6: "Figure 3b plots the relationship between end-of-training L_ΣI values with MIG evaluation scores for both Beamsynthesis and dSprites". But in that figure square box representing dsprites as shown in fig 3a is missing.
3) In eq1, the authors have mentioned DTC(z) = DTC_i(z); I think a few lines describing why that is necessary for the readers.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, the paper seems to be a work in progress, and there are many areas where it needs further improvement. Currently, the work has limited novelty and needs more experiments with results to justify the made claims.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper is about learning disentanglement using scalable non-parametric density estimation. The author proposes a dual total correlation (DTC) metric for disentangling that divides the joint distribution into many low-dimensional conditional distributions. The authors conduct extensive experiments to show the superiority of their approach against state-of-the-art baselines.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength

1) The analysis is shown in section 3, which derives the DTC and its comparison against total correlation. DTC in eqn 1. removes the requirement to compute p(z). DTC requires computing the conditional entropies for disentanglement.
2) Propose a new "Gaussian Channel Autoencoder" (GCAE). As the name suggests, gaussian noise with variance sigma^2 is added to smooth the representations. And in Sec 2, the authors have detailed how they use the discriminator-based method, which applies the density-ratio trick and the Radon-Nikodym theorem to estimate the density of samples from an unknown distribution.
3) The authors conduct experiments on Beamsynthesis (time series dataset) and dsprites (synthetic images) to show GCAE is better than previous baselines. Fig 3a and 3b show the scatter plot of GCAE for summed information loss against mean squared error (MSE) and mutual information gap (MIG). GCAE performs better than baseline models for Beamsynthesis, while in the case of dsprites it does better only for a small range of lambda (hyperparameter of summed information loss).



Weakness

1) The novelty of the paper is limited. The authors claim that using DTC  removes the constraint of estimating p(z), which is difficult compared to the conditional distribution. However, computing "m" conditional distributions needs "m" discriminators, which are sensitive to hyperparameters. It also requires a lot of data and time.
2) The authors use only the MIG metric to show disentanglement. The authors should have compared against other metrics such as DCI, SAP, etc. Please refer to "Measuring Disentanglement: A Review of Metrics" to understand why there is not one disentangling metric and each metric explores different aspects.
3) The datasets used for the experiments are limited. The results work well for Beamsynthesis, not for dsprites. It can be seen in figure 4a and 4b. The scatter plot in figure 3b for beam synthesis shows a negative correlation coefficient of -0.823. There is no mention of dsprites.
4) No qualitative results were shown for dsprites and no results on the latent traversal on these z axes. Since the authors chose m=10 as the latent dimension, and dsprites has five factors of variation, the qualitative result should have helped understand how the disentanglement happened.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The work needs more finetuning and structuring. 
Example:
1) Page 6: "The tightly grouped samples in the lower right of the plot correspond with lambda = 0, and incorporating any lambda > 0 leads to a decrease in L_sumI and increase in MSE. As lambda is increased further the MSE increases only slightly as the average L_sumI decreases significantly." This line is unclear in context with lambda and figure 3a.
2) Page6: "Figure 3b plots the relationship between end-of-training L_ΣI values with MIG evaluation scores for both Beamsynthesis and dSprites". But in that figure square box representing dsprites as shown in fig 3a is missing.
3) In eq1, the authors have mentioned DTC(z) = DTC_i(z); I think a few lines describing why that is necessary for the readers.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, the paper seems to be a work in progress, and there are many areas where it needs further improvement. Currently, the work has limited novelty and needs more experiments with results to justify the made claims.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

### Summary Of The Paper:
The paper introduces the Gaussian Channel Autoencoder (GCAE), a novel method for learning disentangled representations. It aims to address reliability issues observed in existing VAE-based disentanglement techniques by tackling the curse of dimensionality in density estimation within high-dimensional latent spaces. The core of the approach involves leveraging Dual Total Correlation (DTC) instead of Total Correlation, which allows for dividing the joint distribution into many low-dimensional conditional distributions, estimated via multiple discriminator networks. The method incorporates Gaussian noise in the latent space for smoothing and introduces a novel Excess Entropy Power (LEEP) loss for feature stabilization. The authors conduct experiments on Beamsynthesis (time series) and dSprites (synthetic images) datasets, claiming to show the superiority and reliability of their approach against state-of-the-art baselines.

### Strength And Weaknesses:

**Strengths:**

1.  The paper proposes Dual Total Correlation (DTC) as a core metric for disentanglement, which is a novel and theoretically well-motivated approach to overcome the curse of dimensionality prevalent in estimating high-dimensional latent space joint distributions. Section 3 provides an analysis deriving DTC and comparing it against Total Correlation, highlighting that DTC removes the requirement to explicitly compute p(z) by instead computing conditional entropies for disentanglement. This strategic use of DTC via conditional density estimation is considered a highly original contribution.
2.  The authors propose a new "Gaussian Channel Autoencoder" (GCAE) architecture, which is a sound application of established techniques. Gaussian noise with variance sigma^2 is added to smooth the latent representations, a design choice noted as sound for density estimation. Section 2 details the use of a discriminator-based method, applying the density-ratio trick and the Radon-Nikodym theorem to estimate conditional densities. Furthermore, the introduction of the Excess Entropy Power (LEEP) loss as an inductive bias for feature stabilization is also a distinct contribution, with its utility validated through an ablation study.
3.  The authors conduct experiments on two diverse datasets, Beamsynthesis (time series) and dSprites (synthetic images), comparing GCAE against state-of-the-art baselines. While GCAE demonstrates competitive performance and improved reliability on Beamsynthesis, often showing superior and more robust results against leading baselines, its performance on dSprites is more nuanced, showing improvement only for a small range of the lambda hyperparameter (as seen in Figure 3b). The paper also suggests that the proposed LΣI loss could serve as a promising unsupervised indicator for disentanglement success, correlating well with MIG, which is a valuable practical contribution. The experimental evaluation covers multiple disentanglement metrics (MIG, Factor Score, DCI, SAP), and demonstrates robustness to increasing latent space dimensionality in some cases.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  While the core idea of leveraging Dual Total Correlation (DTC) and a collection of conditional distributions to overcome the curse of dimensionality is conceptually novel, the practical implementation introduces significant challenges. The claim that DTC removes the constraint of estimating p(z) (which is difficult) is valid, but computing "m" conditional distributions necessitates training "m" discriminator networks. This multi-discriminator setup is acknowledged to be computationally expensive, requiring significant data and time, and is highly sensitive to hyperparameters like learning rates and optimizer choices, which could pose practical limitations for widespread application and tuning.
2.  While the paper mentions evaluating GCAE using multiple disentanglement metrics (MIG, Factor Score, DCI, SAP) in the text and appendices, the primary disentanglement metric highlighted and visualized in key figures (e.g., Figure 3b) is often MIG. A more comprehensive presentation and discussion of results across all reported metrics, potentially with visual comparisons for DCI and SAP, would strengthen the empirical claims, as different metrics explore varying aspects of disentanglement.
3.  While experiments are conducted on two datasets (Beamsynthesis and dSprites), the consistency of GCAE's performance across them is a concern. The results demonstrate strong performance for Beamsynthesis, but for dSprites, GCAE's superiority over baselines is observed only within a limited range of the lambda hyperparameter (Figure 3b). Furthermore, the analysis of correlations, such as the negative correlation coefficient of -0.823 for Beamsynthesis in Figure 3b, lacks a corresponding discussion or mention for dSprites in the same context, making it difficult to assess overall consistency. This suggests that the method's effectiveness might be dataset-dependent, requiring further investigation across a broader range of datasets to fully justify its generalizability.
4.  A notable omission is the lack of qualitative results for the dSprites dataset, particularly in the form of latent traversals along the learned z axes. Given that dSprites has five known factors of variation and the authors chose a latent dimension of m=10, visualizing how disentanglement manifests qualitatively would significantly enhance understanding of the model's success or failure, especially considering the mixed quantitative results on this dataset.

### Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:

**Clarity**: While the paper's high-level narrative and overall structure are generally clear, the technical clarity is severely compromised by numerous instances of mathematical imprecision, ambiguous formulations, and inconsistent terminology. For example, the statement on Page 6 regarding the relationship between lambda, L_sumI, and MSE in Figure 3a is unclear. Figure 3b is stated to plot dSprites data, but the corresponding square box for dSprites is missing, creating confusion. Furthermore, specific mathematical expressions, such as the density-ratio trick equation in Section 2, lack necessary parentheses for clarity. The definition of the `zm` distribution in Figure 1b's description appears mathematically flawed, and the claim in Section 3 that `h(Zi)` is estimated with `Ez\i pZi(zi|z\i)` is misleading. The `1/(2πe)` factor in the LEEP loss (Eq. 3) is technically inconsistent with typical entropy power definitions, and the role of the `1/n` scaling factor in `LGCAE` (Eq. 4) requires clarification. Inconsistent citation styles and unconventional notation like `νσ` for Gaussian noise further detract from the professional presentation. These issues demand significant effort from the reader to interpret precise technical details and reconcile conflicting statements, particularly between the main text and the appendix. A few lines describing the necessity of `DTC(z) = DTC_i(z)` in Eq. 1 would also aid reader comprehension.

**Quality**: The underlying scientific contribution—the novel application of DTC and conditional density estimation to address the curse of dimensionality—is conceptually strong. The experimental validation is thorough in its scope of baselines and metrics, and it supports claims of competitive performance and robustness in some contexts. However, the quality of the manuscript's presentation, particularly its technical precision and adherence to rigorous mathematical notation, is substantially lacking. The accumulation of these flaws overshadows the strength of the core ideas.

**Novelty**: The paper presents a novel approach to disentanglement learning by effectively leveraging Dual Total Correlation with conditional density estimation, which is a significant departure from traditional VAE-based methods. The introduction of the Excess Entropy Power loss and the demonstrated robustness to latent dimensionality are also novel contributions to the field. However, the practical implications of these novelties are somewhat hindered by the aforementioned implementation and presentation issues.

**Reproducibility**: While the authors state that code is available and provide architectural specifications, hyperparameter settings, and training procedures in the appendices, the numerous ambiguities and inconsistencies in the mathematical descriptions and architectural specifications (e.g., `z_phi` vs. `z` in information functional estimation, ambiguous encoder output for GCAE) would make independent reproduction of the results highly challenging without direct access to and careful reverse-engineering of the provided code. A self-contained, reproducible paper requires unambiguous and consistent technical descriptions.

### Summary Of The Review:
Overall, this paper introduces a conceptually novel approach to disentangled representation learning through the Gaussian Channel Autoencoder and the application of Dual Total Correlation. While the core idea is innovative and the experimental results show promising performance and reliability on certain datasets, particularly Beamsynthesis, the manuscript currently appears to be a work in progress. It is significantly hampered by pervasive mathematical imprecisions, inconsistent notation, and methodological ambiguities that severely impact its clarity, technical soundness, and reproducibility. Furthermore, the limited and sometimes inconsistent performance on dSprites, coupled with the lack of qualitative results, indicates that the claims made require further justification through more robust and extensive experimentation and analysis. The computational cost and hyperparameter sensitivity of the multi-discriminator setup also remain practical concerns. Therefore, extensive revisions are necessary to address these fundamental issues before the work can meet the stringent standards of a high-quality academic publication.

### Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: To address the reliability issue of learning independent representations, the paper proposes to regularize a noisy autoencoder with a new regularization term based on dual total correlation. This results in a new Gaussian Channel Autoencoder, which does not need to estimate the joint distribution of the latent representation and achieves higher disentanglement scores.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

+ It is interesting to use the dual total correlation instead of total correlation as the metric for disentangle representation learning. The metric circumvents the curse of dimensionality of estimating the joint distribution of latent representations.

+ The paper is well organized and easy to follow. The motivation is clear.

Weakness

- The paper claims the method can achieve reliable and high-performing disentanglement outcomes. It seems that only the "reliable" part is supported by higher disentanglement scores from the experiment. What is the definition of "high-performing" and how it is supported by the paper?

- The experimental results are not convincing. As stated in the paper that "there currently aren’t good unsupervised indicators of disentanglement", why only the Mutual Information Gap (MIG) is used as the disentanglement score? How about Z-Diff score [1], SAP score [2], and Factor score [3]?

[1] beta-vae: Learning basic visual concepts with a constrained variational framework. In ICLR, 2017

[2]  Variational inference of disentangled latent concepts from unlabeled observations. In ICLR, 2018

[3]  Disentangling by factorising. In ICML, 2018.

- Some notations are confusing. For example, $DTC(z) = DTC_i(z)$ in Eq. (1). What do $z_{\forall j\neq i}$ and $z_{k\neq j}$ mean and what are their difference? I don't follow  the discussion from $I(z_i; z_{\forall j\neq I})$ in Eq.(2) to $I(z_i;z_j)$.

- The motivation for the major loss term in Eq. (3) is not clear. What is the definition of feature-scale dependent term and why it is helpful?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: Not very clear. The paper has confusing notations. The proposed method is not well explained.

Quality: Some claims are not supported. Experiments are not convincing.

Novelty: The paper contributes some new ideas.

Reproducibility: Good. Key details of the experiments are provided.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper contributes some new ideas to disentangled representation learning. However, some claims are not well supported and the experiments are weak. The writing could also be improved by clearly defining the notations and clarifying some confusing terms.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
To address the reliability issue of learning independent representations, the paper proposes to regularize a noisy autoencoder with a new regularization term based on dual total correlation. This results in a new Gaussian Channel Autoencoder, which does not need to estimate the joint distribution of the latent representation and achieves higher disentanglement scores.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:

+ It is interesting to use the dual total correlation instead of total correlation as the metric for disentangle representation learning. The metric circumvents the curse of dimensionality of estimating the joint distribution of latent representations.

+ The paper is well organized and easy to follow. The motivation is clear.

Weakness

- The paper claims the method can achieve reliable and high-performing disentanglement outcomes. It seems that only the "reliable" part is supported by higher disentanglement scores from the experiment. What is the definition of "high-performing" and how it is supported by the paper?

- The experimental results are not convincing. As stated in the paper that "there currently aren’t good unsupervised indicators of disentanglement", why only the Mutual Information Gap (MIG) is used as the disentanglement score? How about Z-Diff score [1], SAP score [2], and Factor score [3]?

[1] beta-vae: Learning basic visual concepts with a constrained variational framework. In ICLR, 2017

[2]  Variational inference of disentangled latent concepts from unlabeled observations. In ICLR, 2018

[3]  Disentangling by factorising. In ICML, 2018.

- Some notations are confusing. For example, $DTC(z) = DTC_i(z)$ in Eq. (1). What do $z_{\forall j\neq i}$ and $z_{k\neq j}$ mean and what are their difference? I don't follow  the discussion from $I(z_i; z_{\forall j\neq I})$ in Eq.(2) to $I(z_i;z_j)$.

- The motivation for the major loss term in Eq. (3) is not clear. What is the definition of feature-scale dependent term and why it is helpful?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: Not very clear. The paper has confusing notations. The proposed method is not well explained.

Quality: Some claims are not supported. Experiments are not convincing.

Novelty: The paper contributes some new ideas.

Reproducibility: Good. Key details of the experiments are provided.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper contributes some new ideas to disentangled representation learning. However, some claims are not well supported and the experiments are weak. The writing could also be improved by clearly defining the notations and clarifying some confusing terms.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This paper introduces the Gaussian Channel Autoencoder (GCAE), a novel method for learning disentangled representations. GCAE aims to address reliability issues observed in existing VAE-based disentanglement techniques, particularly by circumventing the curse of dimensionality in density estimation within high-dimensional latent spaces. It achieves this by leveraging Dual Total Correlation (DTC) instead of Total Correlation, representing the latent joint distribution as a collection of low-dimensional conditional distributions estimated via multiple discriminator networks. The method also incorporates Gaussian noise in the latent space and introduces a novel Excess Entropy Power (LEEP) loss for feature stabilization. The authors claim that GCAE achieves higher disentanglement scores and can achieve reliable and high-performing disentanglement outcomes. Experiments are presented to demonstrate competitive and reliable disentanglement scores on Beamsynthesis and dSprites datasets, often outperforming state-of-the-art baselines and showing robustness to latent space dimensionality. The paper also proposes LΣI as a promising unsupervised indicator of disentanglement.

**Strength And Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**
The paper presents several commendable strengths. The core idea of using Dual Total Correlation (DTC) as a metric for disentangled representation learning, particularly its application with a collection of conditional distributions to overcome the curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional latent spaces, is highly original and theoretically well-motivated. This approach circumvents the difficulty of estimating the joint distribution of latent representations, which is a significant practical advantage. The introduction of Gaussian noise and the Excess Entropy Power (LEEP) loss as inductive biases for feature stabilization further contributes to the method's novelty. The theoretical argument for DTC over Total Correlation, grounded in the curse of dimensionality, is compelling. The paper is generally well-organized, and its high-level motivation is clear. The empirical evaluation demonstrates that GCAE achieves competitive and reliable disentanglement performance across multiple metrics (including MIG, Factor Score, DCI, and SAP) and two diverse datasets, showing robustness to increasing latent space dimensionality. The inclusion of an ablation study for LEEP helps justify its contribution. Furthermore, the observation that the proposed LΣI correlates with disentanglement metrics offers a valuable step towards unsupervised assessment of disentanglement.

**Weaknesses:**
Despite its strengths, the paper suffers from several significant weaknesses that detract from its overall quality and clarity.

Firstly, while the paper claims the method can achieve reliable and high-performing disentanglement outcomes, it is not entirely clear how "high-performing" is defined or unequivocally supported by the experimental results. While the reliability aspect is generally supported by competitive disentanglement scores and low variance, the convincingness of the "high-performing" claim needs further substantiation. Moreover, the experimental results, despite utilizing multiple metrics, could be more robustly presented or discussed to fully support the paper's claims. Practical limitations also arise from the computational cost associated with training multiple discriminators, which can be significant, especially for very high-dimensional latent spaces. While the authors acknowledge that GCAE can be faster than FactorVAE, the sheer number of discriminators introduces a computational burden. Additionally, the optimization of these discriminators is noted to be sensitive to learning rate and optimizer choices, suggesting potential challenges in practical application and tuning.

Secondly, the manuscript is plagued by mathematical imprecision, inconsistent notation, and ambiguous descriptions, which severely impact its clarity and reproducibility. For example:
*   Some notations are confusing, such as $DTC(z) = DTC_i(z)$ in Eq. (1), and the distinction between $z_{\forall j\neq i}$ and $z_{k\neq j}$ is not clearly explained. The discussion from $I(z_i; z_{\forall j\neq I})$ in Eq.(2) to $I(z_i;z_j)$ also lacks clarity.
*   The motivation for the major loss term in Eq. (3), which includes the LEEP loss, is not sufficiently clear. A deeper theoretical or intuitive explanation of *why* weighting by entropy power leads to feature stabilization would significantly enhance understanding beyond empirical validation. The definition of "feature-scale dependent term" and its helpfulness remains vague.
*   Specific mathematical expressions contain imprecisions, such as the density-ratio trick equation `p_fake(x) = D*(x) p_real(x) / 1-D*(x)` in Section 2, which lacks necessary parentheses. The definition of the `zm` distribution in Figure 1b's description `zm ~ N(µ = (m − 1)− 1 m )` appears mathematically flawed or misstated. The claim in Section 3 that `h(Zi)` is estimated with `Ez\i pZi(zi|z\i)` is misleading, as `h(Zi)` is marginal entropy, not an expectation over conditional densities. The `1/(2πe)` factor in the LEEP loss (Eq. 3) is placed outside the summation, which is technically inconsistent with how entropy power is typically defined per component. The `1/n` scaling factor in `LGCAE` (Eq. 4) needs clarification regarding `n`'s specific role (input or output dimension).
*   Inconsistent notation and terminology are also present, such as unconventional `νσ` for Gaussian noise and vague terms like "dual latent parameters" in experiment details.
*   There are critical inconsistencies in methodological details, particularly in Appendix A.1 regarding the information functional estimation, where it states `z_phi` (noiseless) is used for `z\i` in the expectation `Ez\i~phi_sigma(X)`, which contradicts the `phi_sigma(X)` notation that implies noisy `z`. This significantly impacts reproducibility. The description of "2 × Linear(512, m)" for the GCAE encoder output in Table 1 is ambiguous.

Finally, persistent hyphenation artifacts (e.g., "Gaus- sian", "la- tent") and minor formatting errors detract from the professional presentation of the manuscript.

**Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:**
**Clarity:** While the paper's overall structure and high-level narrative are clear, its clarity is severely compromised by pervasive technical inaccuracies, ambiguous mathematical formulations, and inconsistent terminology. These issues demand significant effort from the reader to interpret the precise technical details and reconcile conflicting statements, particularly between the main text and the appendix. The proposed method is not always clearly explained due to these issues.
**Quality:** The underlying scientific contribution—the novel application of DTC and conditional density estimation—is of high quality, and the experimental validation is thorough and supports the claims of competitive performance and robustness. However, the quality of the manuscript's presentation, particularly its technical precision and adherence to rigorous mathematical notation, is substantially lacking. The accumulation of these flaws overshadows the strength of the core ideas. Some claims are not well-supported by the clarity of the explanations.
**Novelty:** The paper contributes significant new ideas. The central idea of leveraging Dual Total Correlation with conditional density estimation to tackle the curse of dimensionality is highly novel. The introduction of the Excess Entropy Power (LEEP) loss and the demonstrated robustness to latent dimensionality are also novel contributions to the field.
**Reproducibility:** While the paper provides key details of the experiments, detailed architectural specifications, hyperparameter settings, and training procedures in the appendices, and explicitly states that code is available, the numerous ambiguities and inconsistencies in the mathematical descriptions and architectural specifications would make independent reproduction of the results highly challenging without direct access to and careful reverse-engineering of the provided code. A self-contained, reproducible paper requires unambiguous and consistent technical descriptions.

**Summary Of The Review:**
This paper introduces GCAE, a novel disentanglement method utilizing Dual Total Correlation and conditional density estimation to achieve reliable and competitive performance. The core idea is innovative and theoretically well-motivated, and the experimental results demonstrate promising disentanglement scores and robustness. However, the manuscript is plagued by a substantial number of minor yet impactful flaws, including significant mathematical imprecisions, inconsistent notation, and contradictory methodological descriptions. These issues severely hinder the paper's clarity, technical soundness, and reproducibility, overshadowing its otherwise valuable contributions. While the paper contributes new ideas, its claims are not always well-supported, and the writing could be significantly improved by clearly defining notations and clarifying confusing terms and formulations. Extensive revision is required to meet stringent standards of technical precision.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
